summary: Gruber dismisses the latest round of Apple-to-Intel switch rumors, which stem from a Wall Street Journal report citing unnamed industry executives. He acknowledges Apple has legitimate grievances with IBM's G5 production — Steve Jobs promised 3 GHz within a year of WWDC 2003, and two years later they're stuck at 2.7 GHz — but argues the enormous software compatibility hurdles make the switch implausible. The core dilemma: Apple can't pre-announce the switch without killing current hardware sales, but can't spring it without software. He suggests the rumors are more likely planted leaks to pressure IBM, and predicts he'll be writing about the same rumors again in 2007.
key_points: The WSJ sources are unnamed 'industry executives' not confirmed to work at Apple or Intel, making this a rumor column item at best
Software compatibility is the insurmountable hurdle: all Mac OS X software would need recompilation, and emulation of G5 on Intel would be unacceptably slow
Apple faces an impossible marketing paradox — pre-announcing kills current sales, but not pre-announcing means no third-party software at launch
The rumors likely represent planted leaks to pressure IBM into improving G5 production rather than genuine switch plans
Apple could plausibly use Intel chips for non-Mac products like iPods or next-gen AirPort hardware
money_quotes: Every 18-24 months, the tech industry will be captivated by unsubstantiated rumors that Apple is switching the Macintosh to Intel processors.
So, yes, there's a motive for Apple to consider such a switch. But that doesn't make it plausible.
The minute Apple announces they're moving to x86 processors, sales of current hardware dry up. Who's going to spend $3000 for a deprecated CPU architecture?
My advice is to pay no heed whatsoever to any Macintosh-to-Intel rumors that don't address this issue.
The fact that it's technically possible doesn't mean there's a good business case for such a move.
Reading between the lines, I think this is less about whether Apple actually intends to switch processors, and more about planted leaks intended to spur IBM.
My prediction: I'll be writing about this again in 2007.
key_insight: Technical feasibility is not the same as business plausibility — the marketing paradox of pre-announcing a processor switch makes the Apple-Intel rumors far less credible than they appear.
